inventory.name: Waterskin
inventory.description: Boiled and refilled at the last clean spring. Worth more every week the river runs gray.

inventory.name: Trade Tokens
inventory.description: Stamped ceramic rounds honored in Millbrook and grudgingly beyond. The valley's compromise with currency.

inventory.name: Pale Turnip Seedling
inventory.description: A color-drained turnip seedling from the eastern irrigation arm — three mornings of gray-green water have leached it pale. Physical evidence of contamination reaching the soil.

inventory.name: Crystalline Residue Pinch
inventory.description: A fingertip's worth of crystalline powder scraped from the third interface depression at the vent aperture. Nanite-structured, pre-Transition manufacture — holds light briefly rather than reflecting it. Wrapped in a ration-paper fold. Fragile.

deeds.description: Stilled a boiling irrigation argument at the well with a word and a baobab's patience.
Brokered a one-week irrigation truce between Holloway and Renn, reframing a personal dispute as a shared contamination problem for the village elders
Walked the full eastern irrigation arm over two days and located the contamination's entry point — a warm seep beneath an old retaining wall with geometric patterning in the silt below
Traced the six-fold pulse south to Millbrook's drinking wells — confirmed the village water supply is inside the system's reach

turns.summary: A ribbon of purposeful pre-Transition light travels upstream through the irrigation channel and sinks through a hairline crack — glimpsed, unexplained; Aevi goes briefly, brilliantly still.
turns.narration:
The gateline conversation pauses for a breath, and that is when you see it.

Beneath the Lower Terrace's outermost irrigation channel — a narrow stone-cut trough that carries water down from the Heights to the valley farms below — something moves. Not a fish. Not a current. A slow ribbon of light, pale gold shot through with that unmistakable teal, traveling upstream against the water's direction as though it knows where it came from and would like to return. It is about the length of your forearm. It holds its shape. It does not illuminate anything around it; the stone beneath the water stays shadowed. The light is for no one. It does not notice you.

Aevi goes very quiet beside you — not the dimming of wariness, but the perfect stillness of recognition. The motes contract to a single point at your shoulder, brighter than you have ever seen them in full afternoon sun, and hold there without moving for three full breaths. When the ribbon of light reaches a junction-stone in the channel bed, it slows, lingers at a hairline crack in the mortar that runs in a straight line far too precise for any settling, and then — gently, without drama — it sinks through and is gone. The water above the spot runs clear and ordinary. A swallow dips through the space where it was.

Aevi does not speak. After a moment it expands back to its usual loose constellation, and you hear — from just behind your ear, barely above the sound of the channel's flow — a single low hum. A frequency you don't have a name for. Then silence, and the afternoon reasserts itself: the warden's boot scraping stone, the distant rhythm of a hammer somewhere up the terraces, Cellaceron a step to your right.

turns.summary: Usnea crouches at the junction-stone and feels residual warmth pulsing in the vent-aperture rhythm — confirming the ribbon as a node in the same system.
turns.narration:
You drop to a crouch at the channel's edge, one root-tip pressed flat against the mortar beside the junction-stone. The dressed stonework is cool and faintly damp with runoff, but there — beneath that — something else. A warmth that doesn't belong to afternoon sun or spring water. It is faint, diffuse, and patient: not the sharp six-point geometry of the seep, but the same *quality* of heat. A residual impression, as if the ribbon passed this way not once but many times, and the stone has quietly memorized the fact. The crack in the mortar, ruler-straight, runs from channel-bed to the inner face of the junction-stone — too clean for age, too deliberate for settling.

Aevi brightens two degrees and drifts to just above your shoulder, close enough that its motes throw a faint teal wash across the wet stone. It says nothing aloud, but its frequency shifts — a low, almost-below-hearing hum that you have come to recognize as the sound it makes when two things it already knows suddenly rhyme. The warmth under your root-tip pulses once, very faintly, in the rhythm the dockside child tapped: pause, three beats, pause, two beats, pause-pause. Then it stills.

You stay crouched a moment longer, holding the shape of the memory — exactly as you did at the seep, exactly as you did at the vent aperture. The junction-stone is a node in something larger than a single site. The ribbon was not lost. It came here on purpose.

Behind you, the tuning-warden has not moved from the gate arch, but the patience in his posture has shifted into something more watchful. He has been at this gate a long time. He has his chord-staff across both hands now, not threatening — just present.

turns.summary: Usnea names the junction-stone pulse and rhythm plainly; the warden admits he has seen the light before — once, three springs back.
turns.narration:
You rise from the crouch slowly, brushing mortar-dust from your root-tip, and turn to face the warden. He is still watching — chord-staff level, not threatening, but the practiced neutrality is gone. You decide plainness is the right offering here.

"The stone holds warmth," you say, and your voice carries the same even weight you'd use naming a soil temperature to a farmer. "A residual pulse, in the junction mortar. Pause — three beats — pause — two — pause-pause. I've felt that rhythm once before, at a vent aperture on the far slope. Same signature. Whatever moved through this channel came from the same system."

The warden's eyes drop to the channel, then come back to you. Something in his jaw shifts — not surprise exactly, more the quality of a man setting down something he has carried for a while. He does not speak immediately. Above your shoulder Aevi has not dimmed; the teal motes hold their brightness, casting their faint wash on the wet lintel stone, patient as breath. The harmonic vibration in the gate arch seems, for a moment, fractionally lower in pitch — the stone acknowledging nothing, remembering everything.

"Once," the warden says finally. The word costs him something. "Three springs back. Before I had this post." He does not elaborate yet. His grip on the chord-staff has not loosened, but his gaze has moved from your bearing to the hairline crack in the mortar — he can see it from where he stands, and he is not pretending otherwise.

turns.summary: Usnea leaves the Heights gateline and returns to the dock child at the river's edge.
turns.narration:
You give the warden a slow, deliberate nod — the kind that says *this conversation isn't finished, only paused* — and turn from the junction-stone. The gate arch hums its low harmonic as you pass beneath it, dressed stone pressing a faint resonance against your bark. The afternoon light is tilting toward gold now, long shadows pulling east down the terraced switchbacks. Aevi drifts close at your shoulder, still two degrees brighter than usual, teal motes casting that faint wash on the path stones. It says nothing. The hum it carried at the junction fades slowly, like a string releasing tension, not forgetting.

The walk back down to the valley floor takes the better part of an hour — switchbacks, then the packed-dirt lane along the river road, the Echo River murmuring to your left. The water is clear this evening; the gray-green tinge comes and goes with the morning. You file that away. By the time Millbrook's dock planking appears between the willows, the light is the color of old honey and the river smells of cold stone and last year's reeds.

The child is exactly where you last found her: the lowest plank of the dock, legs dangling just above the waterline, palms flat on the wood beside her hips. She doesn't look up when you approach. Eight or nine years old, the particular patience of someone who has stopped expecting adults to come back. Then she registers your roots on the planking — not quite footsteps — and tilts her head a fraction.

"You're the tree," she says, without inflection. A statement of fact. Aevi brightens briefly at that, a small pleased pulse of teal, and you get the impression it approves of children who say what they mean.

turns.summary: Usnea confirms the child's rhythm as real; the child reveals the pattern counts to six and has grown stronger since the water changed.
turns.narration:
You lower yourself to the dock's edge — not crouching exactly, more the careful settling of a very large tree finding its balance on old planking — until you're closer to her level. The river runs clear and cold below, the evening light breaking into copper fragments on the surface. The child still hasn't looked up.

"I think you're hearing something real," you say. Plain as soil temperature. "I found a rhythm like yours at a stone up on the Heights today. Pause — three beats — pause — two beats — pause-pause. The same one."

She looks up then. Not startled — more like someone whose waiting finally resolved into a result. Her eyes move to Aevi, who brightens by a single degree and holds it, patient as a lamp. Then she looks back at you. "The bottom river counts to six," she says. "It goes through the pattern and then it stops for a long time. Then it starts again." She taps it out on the plank with two fingers: the full sequence, then a longer silence, then again. "It's been getting louder since the water went gray. Not louder like sound. Louder like..." She searches for the word and doesn't find it. "More there."

Aevi drifts forward a few inches over the water — closer to the surface than it usually comes — and its motes dim to almost nothing, then return. When it speaks, the voice comes from just behind your left ear, soft and precise: "She is not wrong about the direction. The counting runs upstream."

turns.summary: Usnea invites the dock child to tap the rhythm for the elders Thursday; she agrees provisionally — 'maybe,' which is more than anyone has earned from her before.
turns.narration:
You let the confirmation settle for a moment — the six-count, the growing presence, Aevi still and bright above the water — before you say the next thing. It matters how you say it.

"There's a meeting Thursday," you tell her. "At the common house, when the light goes low. The people who make decisions for Millbrook are going to hear about the river. About what's moving through it." You pause, watching her face. "I'm going to bring what I've found. But what I found in a stone and what you've been hearing from the river — those are two parts of the same thing. I think they should hear it from you too. Just the rhythm. Just what you told me."

She doesn't answer immediately. Her fingers press flat against the dock plank, and for a moment she's listening — you can see it, the way her attention angles inward and downward, toward whatever runs below the clear surface. Then she looks up at you with the particular directness of a child who has been dismissed enough times to have stopped bothering with softness. "They won't believe me," she says. Not complaining. Stating a working theory.

"Mara Wells will be there," you say. "And I'll have already told them the stone at the Heights gate taps the same count. You'd be confirming something they've already heard — not asking them to start from nothing." Aevi drifts a slow half-circle above the water, motes brightening a shade — an endorsement, quiet and precise. The child watches the constellation move, and something shifts in her expression: not trust exactly, but a willingness to revise the working theory. "Maybe," she says finally, which from her sounds like a yes that hasn't been tested yet.

turns.summary: Sela names herself and her mother Pell — the dock child has a name; Mara Wells must secure Pell's permission before Thursday.
turns.narration:
You let the quiet sit for a breath — the river steady below, Aevi holding its bright half-circle above the water — before you ask, easy as asking the time of day: 'What's your name? And your mother's, so Mara can send word to your family before Thursday. Properly.'

She picks at a splinter on the dock plank. 'Sela,' she says. Then, after a moment: 'My mother's Pell. She sells cord and hemp at the east market stall.' A pause. 'She'll say no.' But she says it like reporting a weather fact, not like refusing — as if your getting Pell's permission is now your problem to solve, and she's already moved past it.

Aevi drifts closer to the dock surface, motes settling into a slower, warmer pulse — less the bright attention of discovery, more the patient quality it takes when something difficult is becoming possible. It says nothing. But the hum it carries feels, for just a moment, like approval.

You straighten as much as the low dock allows, roots finding their purchase on the warped planking. 'Then Mara will send proper word to Pell,' you say. 'That's all. You don't have to do anything until Thursday.' Sela nods once, already looking back at the river — her fingers resuming their quiet press against the plank, listening again to whatever counts beneath.

turns.summary: Usnea passes Sela's name and Pell's stall to Mara Wells; Mara commits to approaching Pell at dawn.
turns.narration:
You rise from the dock with the deliberate unhurry of a tree releasing its grip on a fence post — the planking creaks once, settling back into shape. Sela doesn't watch you go. She's already returned to the river, fingers quiet on the wood, listening. Aevi lifts from the water's surface and falls into its usual drift at your shoulder, motes warm and slow.

The walk to the common house takes you through Millbrook's early-evening geography: lamp-oil smell from the chandler's open window, a dog sleeping across a doorstep, the last of the market stalls being folded down. You find Mara Wells at the common house side entrance, where she's speaking with Josen about something to do with Thursday's seating — she sees you coming and wraps the exchange in two sentences.

'Sela,' you say, when you're close enough. 'Eight, nine years old. Her mother is Pell — east market, cord and hemp. Sela expects Pell to refuse. She said it like a fact, not a complaint.' You let Mara take that in. 'Whatever approach you use, she should hear it from someone the market trusts, not a stranger asking her daughter to stand before elders.'

Mara turns the name over once, the way she handles everything — as if testing its weight against something she already knows. 'Pell,' she repeats. A short nod. 'I know which stall. I'll go in the morning, before it opens — easier than after.' She looks at Aevi, whose motes have brightened a single unhurried degree. 'You did the right thing bringing this tonight rather than waiting.' She says it without warmth and without irony, which is how you've learned she means it most.

turns.summary: Mara outlines Thursday's evidence order: physical first, child witness last, and a warning that 'we don't know what it means' must be said plainly.
turns.narration:
Mara listens with the particular economy of someone who has already been turning this problem over in her head for days. When you ask about Thursday's shape, she doesn't consult anything — the plan is already there, behind her eyes.

"Five elders. Councilor Dresh will want the physical evidence first — she trusts what she can hold. The seedling, the vial, the residue pinch if you're willing to part with it briefly. Then your account of the seep and the vent — keep it plain, no Heights politics. Then Aevi." She glances at the motes at your shoulder with the look of someone who has decided to trust something she doesn't fully understand. "After that, the contamination pattern — the six-fold geometry, the junction-stone — and only then Sela. Last. Let the facts do the crediting before a child speaks, so nobody can say it went the other way around."

She smooths her sleeve with a single flat motion. "The weak point is Dresh asking what it means — what the system is *doing*. I don't have an answer for that. If you don't either, say so plainly. The elders can sit with 'we don't know yet' more easily than they can sit with a guess that unravels."

Aevi brightens by a single degree at your shoulder — quiet agreement, or something like it. The common house lamp throws a warm parallelogram across the threshold stones between you.

turns.summary: Usnea settles for the night in Josen's back room at the common house — the planning done, sleep earned.
turns.narration:
You thank Mara with the kind of nod that means *I trust you with this* — not a bow, not a speech, just the slow settling of branches after wind. She receives it the same way: a small, firm dip of her chin, and she's already half-turned back to Josen before you've crossed the threshold.

The common house interior smells of tallow and old grain sacks and the particular warmth of a building that has held sleeping people for generations. Josen — compact, quiet, carrying a lantern — leads you to the back room without ceremony, the way a person does when they've shown a hundred travelers the same door. The straw-tick is thin but dry. The wool blanket is real wool. You fold yourself down into it with the slow patience of deep roots finding stone, branches settling, the long work of the day releasing through your heartwood.

Aevi dims almost to nothing — three faint motes moving in a slow triangle near the ceiling, a nightwatch rhythm you've come to recognize as the motes' version of sleep. The lamp-oil light fades as Josen withdraws. Outside, Millbrook is quiet: a dog's single bark, the river's permanent voice, the creak of a water wheel making its patient rounds. Deep night, early spring, the cold just beginning to ease at the edges.

You lie in the dark and let Thursday sort itself. The plan is as good as it can be tonight. Mara will be at Pell's stall at dawn. Sela will or won't come. The elders will or won't listen. The six-fold system will continue its patient work regardless of any of it — and that, above everything else, is the fact that keeps finding you at the edge of sleep.

turns.summary: Aevi admits two things: the pulse has entered the river current itself, and the junction-stone recognized it — something it cannot explain.
turns.narration:
The candle has given up the last of itself — just a thread of smoke and the smell of hot tin. In the dark, Aevi's three slow motes trace their nightwatch ellipse near the ceiling, teal-dim, patient as stars. You settle against the straw-tick and let your branches go slack, the way you do when you're not trying to hold anything up.

"Twice today," you say quietly, not an accusation — more like naming a thing you noticed the way you notice the angle of light changing. "Over the dock water. Then at the gateline. You went very small."

A long pause. One mote drifts down from the ceiling and hovers near your shoulder, closer than the nightwatch rhythm usually brings it. When Aevi speaks, its voice comes from just behind your ear, lower than usual — the hum of something remembering rather than reporting. "The water at the dock carries the pulse now. Not faintly. It is in the current." Another pause. "At the gateline — the stone. It recognized me. I don't know what that means. I'm not sure I like that I don't know."

The mote sits there, small and still, not dimming further but not brightening either. Aevi's uncertainty is a rare thing — it usually only names what it is certain of. You let the silence hold it for a moment, the way good soil holds a root without squeezing. Outside, the river runs on, constant and gray-green, doing whatever it is doing in the dark. Thursday is two days away. Sleep, when it comes, is heavy and full, the kind that takes the whole body at once.

turns.summary: Usnea makes room on the straw-tick; Aevi settles close and dims to near-stillness — the night deepens into shared quiet.
turns.narration:
You shift on the straw-tick, making room — not much room, but the gesture is clear. You hold one broad hand out, palm up, open to the dark. An invitation without words, the way you'd make space beneath your canopy for a bird that had been flying all day.

Aevi is quiet for a long moment. Then the three motes drift down from the ceiling — slowly, unhurried — and settle near the hollow of your shoulder, tucked against the curve where bark meets the wool blanket. Closer than they ever sit in daylight. The teal light is so dim it barely registers, just a faint warmth at the edge of vision, like embers banked for the night.

You close your eyes. The river speaks through the wall — constant, unchanged on its surface, carrying whatever it carries in its depths. You breathe out, let the roots of you go still, and Aevi's motes slow their drift until they are nearly motionless. Not sleeping, exactly. But resting, in whatever way a constellation of patient motes rests — present, close, and quiet.

Dawn will come. Mara will approach Pell at the east market stall before the cords are even laid out. Thursday waits. For now, the common house holds the dark, and you hold nothing at all.

turns.summary: Usnea rises at first light, reaches the dock before Sela, and feels the six-fold pulse riding the current itself — the system has expanded overnight.
turns.narration:
The river wakes you before the village does. Not sound — the river has been sound all night — but a change in it, a new undertone pressing through the plank wall like a second heartbeat finding its footing. You rise without deliberation, the way a tree responds to first light: not a decision, just a turning. Aevi stirs at your shoulder, the three motes brightening from banked-ember to steady teal as you ease the iron latch and step out into the lane.

Millbrook at this hour is blue and hushed. The eastern sky holds the faintest membrane of pale gold — not sunrise yet, just the promise of it — and the dew on the cobbles catches it in a thousand small mirrors. Your roots find the familiar path to the dock without needing your eyes. The pulse reaches you before you reach the water. It is different. Yesterday it was a thing you had to press down through soil to find; now it rides the current like a note sustained in a bell that has not stopped ringing. You stand at the dock's edge and let it move through your root-tips, and it is *there* — present, rhythmic, unhurried, patient as old machinery obeying old instructions. Pause. One. Two. Three. Pause. Four. Five. Pause-pause. The count is unmistakable, and it is LOUD in the way that matters: not volume, but weight. The system has expanded its presence, Aevi told you, and your roots are the proof.

Aevi expands to its widest morning ring, a bright halo of teal motes fanning out over the water's surface — and then holds there, very still, in a way it almost never does. It does not speak yet. It is listening to something you can only feel in parts. Sela is not at the dock. The lowest plank is empty, still damp from last night's dew, a small heel-print still soft in the mud at its edge — she was here earlier, or very recently, and left. But the print is fresh, and the water tells you what it has to tell regardless: the six-fold pattern is no longer below the river. It *is* the river, or at least it is learning to be.

turns.summary: Usnea holds at the dock's edge and reads the six-fold pulse — steady, not accelerating, but cleaner and more declarative than yesterday.
turns.narration:
You lower yourself to the dock's edge and let your roots spread wide across the wet planks, finding the gaps between boards where the river breathes upward. The pulse arrives without ceremony — it has been there, you realize, since you woke; you were simply too busy moving to receive it fully. Now you stop. Now you listen the way a tree listens: with everything, without hurry.

The six-count is there. Pause — one, two, three — pause — four, five — pause-pause. And again. And again. It is not faster than yesterday, not louder in the way sound is loud, but there is a quality of *settling* to it, the way a voice drops when it has stopped trying to be heard and simply begins to speak. Whatever is running this rhythm, it is not escalating. It is arriving. Becoming present in the current the way the current is present in the river — not an intrusion, a condition.

Aevi fans wider over the water, motes splayed at their full morning ring, and says nothing for a long time. Then, very quietly, from just behind your left ear: "Holding steady. The count has not changed. But the signal is — cleaner. Less like a question. More like a statement." A pause. "I don't know if that is better or worse."

The heel-print in the dock mud catches the pale gold of the not-yet-risen sun. Sela was here before you. She felt this too, probably, the way she always has — not through roots but through the soles of her feet on the lowest plank, the river talking up through the wood into her bones. You find that you are glad she came. You find that you want Pell to say yes.

turns.summary: Usnea traces the six-fold pulse upstream past the mill weir — it extends at least three hundred paces and is still spreading; Aevi confirms it was only under the dock four days ago.
turns.narration:
You don't rise from the dock's edge — you extend. Roots push along the wet planks, find the gap where dock meets bank, and sink into the muddy verge without ceremony. The pulse is immediate. You let it run you northward like a current finding its grade, your awareness sliding along the riverbank in the way water finds low ground — not a search, but a following.

The six-count rides the Echo River all the way to the first bend, where the bank curves and the willows dip their finger-roots into the shallows. Still there. You press further, reaching the old stone weir that divides the mill-race from the main channel — and the pulse crosses it. Both sides. It has no preference for the mill-race or the river proper; it moves through water the way a chord moves through air, indifferent to the vessel. You feel the moment your reach begins to thin, somewhere just past the weir's northern face, where a gravel bar breaks the current into braids — the pulse doesn't stop there, but it grows harder to distinguish from the river's own texture, the way a voice becomes hard to find in a crowd without disappearing entirely. Further than you expected. Much further.

Aevi pulls tight at your shoulder without quite touching you, its motes arranging themselves into a narrow filament — the pattern it makes when something has confirmed a suspicion rather than opened a question. When you finally draw your roots back and sit up, blinking against the gold that has begun to properly breach the eastern ridge, it speaks: "Past the weir." A pause. "When I first tasted it, it was only under the dock. That was four days ago." It does not say what that implies. It doesn't need to.

The river runs below you, silver-gray in the morning light, carrying the rhythm you now know reaches at least three hundred paces upstream — and possibly further still. Thursday is two days away. The elder meeting will need this: not just a pulse in one place, but a pulse that has a direction, a spread, a memory of when it wasn't there.

*You feel it settle into you — a new steadiness. (Level 3: +1 attunement, +5 reserves, and growth to choose — a sub-attribute point and a skill point.)*

turns.summary: Six-fold pulse confirmed south toward Millbrook's drinking wells — the system's reach now encompasses the village water supply.
turns.narration:
You don't lift your roots — you redirect them. The pulse has been running you north all morning; now you let it run you south, reversing the current of your attention the way a tree turns leaves when the light shifts. Your roots press deeper into the bank mud, past the cold surface layer, into the waterlogged dark where the river's underside speaks plainly.

The surge takes you further than you intended. The six-count floods your awareness — not just the signal, but the shape of the whole system at once, the way a bell's resonance fills a room rather than traveling across it. You feel the pulse pass under the dock, past the lowest plank where Sela's heel-print still holds its shape in the mud, and continue south without interruption. The village side. It flows through the loam beneath the west-bank gardens, follows the buried clay pipes that feed Millbrook's drinking wells, and arrives there — steady, patient, already present. The wells are inside the signal. They have been for some time; the mud's thermal record says so, and today the record is impossible to misread. This is not contamination arriving. This is contamination settled in.

Aevi's filament at your shoulder thickens into its full constellation — three motes becoming a dozen, teal brightening toward white at the center. It doesn't speak immediately. It hums a low frequency, the same one it uses near Precursor workmanship, and then says, simply: *Both directions. The pulse does not begin at the dock. The dock is a middle.* A pause, its motes still. *The wells are downstream of a node. Whatever the system is preparing, the preparation includes Millbrook's water supply. All of it.*

You pull your roots back slowly, and the morning snaps back into ordinary scale: gold light cresting the ridge-line now, the first smoke rising from Millbrook's chimneys, the smell of damp wood and early bread. The six-fold pulse continues in the current below, unhurried and unconcerned with what you've just understood. Thursday's meeting is two days away. The evidence you've gathered was enough for yesterday; this changes what the elders are walking into.

turns.summary: Usnea reaches Mara Wells at the east market and delivers the well-contamination news mid-conversation — Pell is listening differently now.
turns.narration:
You retract your roots from the bank mud in one long exhale and rise to your full height, bark warm from the eastern light. Aevi drifts up beside you without being asked — reading your intention, condensing toward the brighter end of its range. The dock is quiet except for the river and the first creak of wagon-wheels somewhere up the market lane.

The east market is only a few minutes' walk from the dock, past the well-square and the row of storage sheds. You know where Pell's cord stall sits — Sela described it exactly. You find Mara Wells there before the stall is even properly open: she is standing at the corner of the canvas awning, speaking in a low, patient voice to a compact woman with coiled rope samples laid across one arm. Pell. She has Mara's number — you can see it in the set of her jaw, the way she's holding the rope like a shield — but she hasn't said no yet.

Mara glances at you over Pell's shoulder. Something in your bearing stops the conversation more cleanly than an interruption would. You step in close enough to speak quietly, and you say it plain: the pulse is in the drinking wells. Not only the river, not only the irrigation arm — the wells the whole village draws from every morning. Mara goes still. Her hand, which was reaching toward Pell in careful social gesture, drops to her side. Pell reads the stillness too and looks between you both.

Mara turns back to Pell and says, without raising her voice: *'Your daughter hears something the rest of us are only beginning to feel. She's not wrong. And the elders need to hear it before Thursday becomes too late to matter.'* It isn't the speech she'd planned — it's shorter, and truer. Pell's grip on the rope samples shifts. She hasn't said yes. But she is listening now in a different way.

turns.summary: Usnea named Sela's danger plainly to Pell — and the directness landed wrong; Pell's fear curdled into protective anger before permission could be asked.
turns.narration:
You step forward past Mara's shoulder before the better part of you can reconsider, and you speak to Pell directly — root-to-earth, no ceremony. You tell her what you found at the dock this morning. Not the elders, not the meeting, not the evidence order. You tell her the pulse runs under the well her daughter drinks from. You tell her the six-count is in the village water supply. You tell her Sela has been feeling this for weeks from the dock's lowest plank while the rest of Millbrook looked away, and that makes Sela the person in this village who has been living closest to whatever is coming.

You mean it as truth spoken plain. It lands as something else.

Pell's grip on the coiled rope goes white-knuckled. Her chin drops, not in acquiescence but in the specific way a parent's face closes when someone has named their child's danger aloud in a public place before they were ready to hear it. 'You're telling me my daughter has been sitting on a dock above something that reaches my wells,' she says, and the flatness in it is not a question. Mara goes very still at your elbow. The east market lane is almost empty but not quite — a cart has stopped somewhere behind you, and the man steadying the horse is absolutely not looking over.

The backlash is not a sound or a sensation you can name exactly. It is the way the morning light seems to reject your presence for a half-second — your bark going cold where it was warm, a deep ache running from your crown-roots down through your lower branches, as if you pushed too much of yourself into the words and the words were thrown back. Aevi dims sharply, pulling inward to a single bright mote at your shoulder. The energy cost runs through you like a winter-current. Pell is not wrong to be angry. You are not wrong about the wells. And those two truths are currently occupying the same space with no room between them.

turns.summary: Usnea stepped back; Mara reclaimed the thread — Pell is listening, still guarded, not yet open.
turns.narration:
You take one deliberate step back — not retreat, just room-making. Your bark settles, your branches still, and you let the silence you created belong to Mara now. Aevi dims to almost nothing at your shoulder, reading the same cue: this is not your moment to speak.

Mara moves into the space you've left. She doesn't reach for Pell or soften her voice into something careful — she simply stands where she is and lets the anger be what it is for a breath. Then she says, quietly, 'You're right to be angry. We came here talking about evidence and meetings and didn't start with what matters. Sela matters.' She doesn't rush the next sentence. 'We're not asking her to carry this. We're asking the elders to carry it — so she doesn't have to anymore.'

Pell's grip on the rope doesn't loosen, but her jaw shifts. She's still closed, still white-knuckled — but she's listening in the way a person listens when they're deciding whether to stay angry or let something land. The cart driver behind you has gone very still, the way people do when they realize they're witnessing something real.

Mara holds the quiet. She doesn't fill it. The damp canvas awning drips once onto the lane stones between you all.

turns.summary: Usnea quietly asked whether the Thursday meeting could move — it cannot, but Mara named the open-door rule; Pell is now asking about the order of speakers.
turns.narration:
You lean toward Mara just slightly — not a whisper, not quite open address — and let your voice carry to where Pell stands without aiming it at her directly. 'Mara. The common house on Thursday — is that fixed, or could it move somewhere Pell might come herself? See how the elders handle Sela before she has to be in it?' You keep your eyes on the middle distance, not on Pell, not performing it.

Mara doesn't answer immediately. She turns the question over once, visibly — you can see her running the political math behind her eyes, the five elders, Dresh's preference for the common house's table and its particular authority. Then she says, flat and honest: 'Dresh won't shift the venue. He anchors at the common house.' A pause. 'But the elders meet in the open room, not the back. Anyone in Millbrook can stand at the door.'

Pell's grip on the rope loosens a fraction — not release, but recalibration. Her eyes move from Mara to you and back, and something in the set of her jaw changes: not softer, but less purely closed. She is working out what 'stand at the door' means for her. Whether it means she can pull Sela out. Whether it means she keeps control.

She hasn't said yes. She hasn't said no. But she asks, quietly and without warmth: 'Who else speaks before her?'
